ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for pancakes with honey; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of two medium-sized pancakes (approximately 120 g total) drizzled with honey, commonly consumed as a snack or light breakfast.

nutrient_source.title: Pancakes with honey — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Pancakes with honey' (record F11100018). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical source at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Pancake, plain, made from mix — GI value 58 (Atkinson et al., 2008); analogous estimate applied to pancakes with honey
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 (medium) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain pancakes made from mix (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, AJCN 2008). Honey has a GI of approximately 55–58 (University of Sydney GI Database). The composite GI of 58 for pancakes with honey is a defensible analogous estimate consistent with both components. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 43.7) / 100 ≈ 25.3 for a 120 g serving (carbohydrate_g per serving = 36.41 × 1.2 = 43.69 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.3 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.69 g/100 g) and very low potassium (7 mg/100 g), promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention and unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid pancakes with honey due to the high sugar load (13.3 g/100 g) and negligible potassium (7 mg/100 g), which together impair blood pressure regulation; if consumed, restrict to a small portion and pair with potassium-rich foods such as banana or low-fat yogurt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (36.41 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.69 g/100 g) and medium GI (58), combined with honey-derived fructose-glucose that rapidly elevates postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pancakes with honey are not recommended for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to the high glycaemic load (GL ≈ 25 per serving), low fibre content, and honey's rapid glucose-raising effect; if consumed occasionally, limit to one small pancake, omit or minimise honey, and pair with a protein source to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate energy density (238 kcal/100 g), high sugar content (13.3 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (1.69 g/100 g) with limited micronutrient density relevant to cardiovascular protection
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, pancakes with honey should be consumed infrequently and in small portions, as the high sugar and low fibre profile contributes to dyslipidaemia risk and weight gain; replacing honey with fresh berries and using wholegrain flour would substantially improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 13.3 g/100 g with low fibre (1.69 g/100 g) and absence of omega-3 fatty acids or meaningful anti-inflammatory polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit pancakes with honey as the refined sugar and low fibre content may promote pro-inflammatory pathways; substituting honey with anti-inflammatory toppings such as walnuts or turmeric-spiced yogurt is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pancakes with honey is a popular snack and breakfast item consisting of plain wheat-flour pancakes — made from a batter of flour, egg, milk, and a small amount of fat — cooked on a griddle or pan until golden, and drizzled with honey before serving. The texture is soft and slightly spongy with a mildly sweet flavour enhanced by the floral sweetness of honey. Per 100 g, the dish provides 238 kcal, 7.04 g protein, 6.93 g fat, 36.41 g carbohydrate, 13.3 g sugar, 1.69 g dietary fibre, and 83 mg sodium. The medium glycaemic index (GI 58) combined with a high carbohydrate load results in a substantial glycaemic load (approximately 25 per 120 g serving), making this food unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension without careful portion control. The low fibre content (1.69 g/100 g) and high sugar from honey limit its nutritional value for metabolic health. Calcium content is notable at 89 mg/100 g, contributed by the milk in the batter. This dish is widely consumed internationally and is classified as a processed snack food. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable. Clinically, the combination of refined carbohydrates, added honey sugar, and low fibre warrants caution for individuals with diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat pancakes with honey?
How many calories in pancakes with honey?
Is honey on pancakes bad for blood pressure?
What is the GI of pancakes with honey?
Is pancake with honey suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F11100018) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 58 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain pancakes, which is the closest defensible approved-source match. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for pancakes with honey is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11100018 as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this record. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source F11100018; pancakes made without egg yolk or with minimal egg may report zero cholesterol. potassium_mg reported as 7.0 mg/100 g per HPB source — this is an unusually low value and may reflect the specific formulation tested; retained as reported from the approved source. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain pancakes from mix); honey component GI is also approximately 55–58 per the same database, supporting the composite estimate. This food is not a Singaporean dish and therefore multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean cuisine (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply; the dish is classified as Western/International.
